lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
There is nothing inconsistent in praising a man for some things and blaming him in others. You have done it in my case.

title: There is nothing inconsistent in praising a man for some things and blaming him in others. You have done it in my case.

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In reference to your alternate praise and disparagement of me, you argue with great acuteness that you have the same right to speak good and evil of me that I have to find fault with Origen and Didymus whom I once praised.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I must instruct you, then, wisest of men and chief of Roman dialecticians, that there is no fault of logic in praising a man in certain respects while you blame him in others, but only in approving and disapproving one and the same thing.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I will take an example, so that, though you may not understand, the wise reader may join me in understanding the point.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In the case of Tertullian we praise his great talent, but we condemn his heresy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In that of Origen we admire his knowledge of the Scriptures, but nevertheless we do not accept his false doctrine.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to Didymus, however, we extol both his powers of memory, and the purity of his faith in the Trinity, while on the other point in which he erred in trusting to Origen we withdraw from him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The vices of our teachers are not to be imitated, their virtues are.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: There was a man at Rome who had an African, a very learned man, as his grammar teacher; and he thought that he was rising to an equality with his teacher because he copied his strident voice and his faulty pronunciation.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You in your Preface to the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν speak of me as your brother and call me your most eloquent colleague, and proclaim my soundness in the faith.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: From these three points you cannot draw back; carp at me on all other points as you please, so long as you do not openly contradict this testimony which you bear to me; for in calling me friend and colleague, you confess me worthy of your friendship; when you proclaim me an eloquent man, you cannot go on accusing me of ignorance; and when you confess that I am in all points a catholic, you cannot fix on me the guilt of heresy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Beyond these three points you may charge me with anything you like without openly contradicting yourself.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: From all this calculation the net result is that you are wrong in blaming in me what you formerly praised; but that I am not in fault when, in the case of the same men, I praise what is laudable and blame what is censurable.
